content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
IN PART

February 5, 1987 by: 95-213

James R. Christiansen
1607 Chapala Street
Santa Barbara, CA 93101-2911

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-87-019

Dear Mr. Christiansen:

You have requested advice on behalf of June Christensen, a
member of the City Council of Solvang, regarding her duties
under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Your letter states only a general
question; it does not seek advice concerning a specific
decision pending before the city council. Therefore, we
consider it to be a request for informal assistance pursuant to
Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2/

FACTS

Ms. Christensen's husband is a 50-percent partner in an
accounting partnership. He owns his 50-percent share in the
partnership through a professional corporation, of which he
owns 100 percent.3/

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California
Administrative Code Section 18000, et seq. All references to
Regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California
Administrative Code.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

3/ We have assumed that there is no separate property
agreement between Ms. Christensen and her husband. Your letter
does not mention such an agreement; therefore, this letter is
based on the assumption that any investments and income in
question are community property.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

James R. Christiansen
February 5, 1987
Page 2


QUESTIONS

1. When must Ms. Christensen disclose the names of her
husband's clients on her statement of economic interests?

2. When must Ms. Christensen disqualify herself from
participating in a decision before the city council that could
affect her husband's client?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Ms. Christensen must disclose on her statement of
economic interests the name of any client of her husband's
accounting partnership who has provided $40,000 or more in
gross receipts to the partnership during the calendar year.

2. Ms. Christensen must disqualify herself from
participating in any decision which would foreseeably and
materially affect a client of her husband's accounting
partnership who has provided or promised $1,000 or more in
gross receipts to the partnership during the 12 months
preceding the decision, if the effect of the decision on the
client would be distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.

ANALYSIS

Disclosure

Your first question concerns disclosure of income. Income
is broadly defined in the Act. With certain exceptions, income
includes salary, wages and other payments, including any
community property interest in the income of a spouse. Income
of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income
of any business entity in which the individual or spouse owns a
10-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030.)

Section 87207(a) states the general rule that city
councilmembers must disclose each source of income aggregating
$250 or more in value. However, Section 87207(b) provides a
higher threshold for reporting an official's pro rata share of
income to a business entity. Section 87207(b) provides:

(b) When the filer's pro rata share of income to
a business entity, including income to a sole
proprietorship, is required to be reported under this
article, the statement shall contain:

(1) The name, address, and a general description
of the business activity of the business entity;

James R. Christiansen
February 5, 1987
Page 3


(2) The name of every person from whom the
business entity received payments if the filer's pro
rata share of gross receipts from such person was
equal to or greater than ten thousand dollars
($10,000) during a calendar year.

Thus, Ms. Christensen is required to disclose the name of
her husband's accounting clients only if her pro rata share of
the gross receipts from a particular client were $10,000 or
more during the calendar year. Ms. Christensen's pro rata
share of the gross receipts from the accounting partnership is
based on her community property interest in her husband's
income. Since he is a 50-percent owner of the accounting firm,
50 percent of its gross receipts are attributed to him. Fifty
percent of those receipts are then attributed to
Ms. Christensen as her pro rata share. Thus, one-fourth of the
gross receipts of the firm are attributed to her. Therefore,
if a client provides the accounting partnership with $40,000 or
more in gross receipts in a calendar year, Ms. Christensen's
share is $10,000 and she must disclose the name of the client
on her statement of economic interests (Form 721).4/
Ms. Christensen is not required to disclose the name of any
clients who have provided less than $40,000 to the partnership
during the calendar year.

You should also note that Ms. Christensen will be required
to report her husband's professional corporation on Schedule A
of Form 721. She must report on Schedule C-2 an interest in
the accounting partnership.5/ She must also disclose her
husband's professional corporation as a source of income on
Schedule C if her husband's pro rata share of the corporation's
gross receipts is $500 or more, one-half of which would be
attributed to her.

Disqualification

Your second question concerns Ms. Christensen's duty to
disqualify herself from participating in governmental decisions

4/ Because the clients are sources of income to the
partnership and her husband's corporation, Ms. Christensen may
list the partnership and the corporation together (i.e. name of
corporation/name of partnership) on the line for "Name of
Business Entity" on Schedule H-2 of Form 721.

5/ Investments held directly or indirectly by an
official's spouse are considered investments of the official.
(Section 82034.) The value to be reported is Ms. Christensen's
husband's ownership interest in the business entities.

James R. Christiansen
February 5, 1987
Page 4


when she has a conflict of interest. Section 87100 prohibits
any public official from making, participating in, or using her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
she knows or has reason to know she has a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on, among other things, any source of income
totaling $250 or more paid to, received by, or promised to the
official during the 12 months preceding the decision. (Section
87103(c).)

     Accordingly, if an official has received at least $250 in
income from a single source, the income may provide the basis
for the official's disqualification from decisions affecting
the source of the income. In Ms. Christensen's case, we are
dealing with income received by or promised to her husband.
Ms. Christensen's interest in her husband's income is based on
her community property (i.e., 50-percent) interest. Therefore,
her husband's pro rata share of the gross receipts from a
client must be over $500 before Ms. Christensen's community
property share is $250. Her husband is a 50-percent owner of
the accounting partnership; therefore, his pro rata share of
the gross receipts from any particular client is deemed to be
50 percent of the total paid. Accordingly, a client must pay
the accounting partnership at least $1,000 before
Ms. Christensen's share of the income will equal $250. If a
client has paid or promised the accounting partnership $1,000
or more during the 12 months preceding a city council decision,
Ms. Christensen must determine whether the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the client
that is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

     In your letter, you did not discuss any specific decisions
pending before the city council that might affect one of the
clients of Ms. Christensen's husband's accounting partnership.
Therefore, we can provide only the following general guidance
with regard to Ms. Christensen's duty to disqualify herself
from participating in city council decisions. If, at a future
date, you have a question about a specific decision, please
feel free to contact this office.

     When a decision comes before the city council which could
affect one of the accounting firm's clients who has paid or
promised the firm $1,000 or more during the preceding 12
months, it is first necessary to determine whether the client
will be foreseeably and materially affected by the decision.
The effect of a decision is considered reasonably foreseeable
if there is a "substantial likelihood" that it will occur.

James R. Christiansen
February 5, 1987
Page 5


Certainty is not required; however, if the effect is but a mere
possibility, is is not reasonably foreseeable. (Thorner
Opinion, 1 FPPC Ops. 198 (No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975), copy
enclosed.)

     The Commission has adopted guidelines for determining
whether the effect of a decision will be deemed material.
Regulation 18702.1(a)(1) (copy enclosed) provides that the
effect of a decision is material if any person, including a
business entity, which has been a source of income to the
official of $250 or more in the preceding 12 months, appears
before the official in connection with the decision. A person
or business entity "appears before" an official in connection
with a decision when that person or business entity, either
personally or through an agent:

          (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
     decision will be made by filing an application, claim,
     appeal, or similar request; or

          (2) Is a named party in the proceeding
     concerning the decision before the official or the
     body on which the official serves.

                    Regulation 18702.1(b).

     For example, if Ms. Christensen's husband's client has paid or
promised to pay the accounting partnership $1,000 or more in
the past 12 months, and then appears before the city council by
filing for a conditional use permit, the effect of the decision
on the client would be considered material.

     Some decisions will affect the clients of the accounting
partnership even though they are not the applicants for, or
parties to, the decision. For example, if the city council is
considering raising the fees for building permits within the
city, that decision may affect one or more of the clients of
the accounting partnership. If the client is a business
entity, the monetary guidelines contained in Regulation 18702.2
(copy enclosed) apply for purposes of determining whether the
effect will be considered material.

     The guidelines in Regulation 18702.2 vary with the size of
the business entity. It appears from your letter that in most
cases the clients who are business entities would be relatively
small businesses. In that case, if the building permit fee
decision would increase or decrease the client's expenses by
$2,500 or more in a fiscal year, the effect is considered

James R. Christiansen
February 5, 1987
Page 6


material. (Regulation 18702.2(g)(2).) If the decision were
one which would foreseeably affect the gross revenues of the
client, or its assets or liabilities, an increase or decrease
of $10,000 in a fiscal year is considered material.
(Regulation 18702.2(g)(1) and (3).)

There are no similar monetary guidelines for determining
whether the effect of a decision on a client who is an
individual will be considered material. In that case, if the
effect on the individual is "significant," it will be
considered material. (Regulation 18702(a), copy enclosed.)

If Ms. Christiansen determines that one of her husband's
clients has paid $1,000 or more to the partnership during the
preceding 12 months, and that client would be foreseeably and
materially affected by a decision pending before the city
council, she must disqualify herself from participating in the
decision unless the effect of the decision on the client would
not be distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. The effect of a governmental decision on an
official's interests is distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally unless the decision will affect the official's
interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all
members of the public or a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703, copy enclosed.) For Ms. Christiansen, the
"public" is the population of the city of Solvang. (See Owen
Opinion, 2 FPPC Ops. 77, 81 (No. 76-005, June 2, 1976), copy
enclosed.)

If a decision would affect Ms. Christiansen's husband's
client in substantially the same manner as it would affect the
entire City of Solvang, or a significant segment of the city,
then Ms. Christiansen may participate in the decision. For
example, a decision to impose a city sales tax, which will
affect all residents of the city, does not affect any
individual client in a different manner than it affects the
public generally. As a result, Ms. Christiansen could
participate in the decision.

In summary, Ms. Christiansen must first determine whether
the decision would directly or indirectly affect any client of
her husband's accounting partnership who has paid or promised
to pay $1,000 or more in the past 12 months. If the decision
could foreseeably and materially affect the client,
Ms. Christiansen must disqualify herself from participating in
the decision, unless the effect on the client would be
substantially the same as the effect on the public generally.

James R. Christiansen
February 5, 1987
Page 7


In your letter, you expressed concern that Ms. Christensen's
husband is unwilling to disclose to his wife the details of his
accounting practice. His reluctance is reportedly due to the
confidential nature of his business and the small size and
relative isolation of Solvang. You proposed the following
method of ensuring that Ms. Christensen will know when to
disqualify herself from a decision affecting one of her
husband's clients:

     1. The city staff would prepare in advance a list of
those applicants who are expected to appear on the next
city council agenda.

     2. She would give that list to her husband prior to
the meeting. Her husband would have his client list and
the partnership income reviewed with due diligence by a
member of the partnership's staff as to the threshold level
of income (i.e., $1,000). Ms. Christensen would then be
advised whether any of those on the list had reached the
threshold level.

     3. She would disqualify herself for all clients that
were reported to her as exceeding the threshold of $1,000.

Your proposed method of determining which clients could
create potential conflicts of interest is basically a good
approach. We have a few comments, however. First, as we
discussed above, city council decisions may directly or
indirectly affect more than the applicants. For example, if a
decision were to affect real property adjacent to property
owned by one of Ms. Christensen's husband's clients, and the
effect on the client's real property would be foreseeable,
material, and distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, then Ms. Christensen would be required to disqualify
herself from participating in the decision. Therefore, we
suggest that the city staff provide Ms. Christensen with a
description of the subject matter of the decisions pending
before the city council, in addition to providing a list of the
applicants. You should note, however, that when a client is
affected by a decision, but is not the applicant,
Ms. Christensen's disqualification would not be automatic. Her
disqualification is required only if there would be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the client,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

As you discussed in your letter, Ms. Christensen is
required to disqualify herself from participating in a decision
if she knows or has reason to know of her financial interest in

James R. Christiansen
February 5, 1987
Page 8


the decision. (Section 87100.) The approach you have
suggested would be evidence of Ms. Christensen's good faith
effort to comply with the disqualification requirements of the
Act.5/ Nevertheless, Ms. Christensen should be informed that
her responsibility for disclosure and disqualification cannot
be transferred to a staff person in her husband's firm. If she
becomes aware of her husband's clients through other means,
such as during a social function or during conversations with
her husband, she may also know or have reason to know of her
financial interest in a particular decision.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By: Kathryn E. Donovan
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

KED:plh
Enclosures

_____________________
5/ The disclosure of clients of the partnership who have
provided $40,000 or more in gross receipts is not dependent
upon whether Ms. Christensen "knows or has reason to know" of
the amount paid by the clients.

JAMES R. CHRISTIANSEN
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW
1607 CHAPALA STREET
SANTA BARBARA, CALIFORNIA 93101-2911
TELEPHONE 962-8141
(AREA CODE 805)

December 31, 1986


Fair Political Practices Commission
Suite 800
428 "J" Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Attn.: Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
       Technical Assistance and Analysis Division

Dear Sirs:

     I am writing to you concerning avoiding a potential conflict
of interest problem of a newly elected councilperson, June
Christiansen, in the small City of Solvang where her is engaged
in the business of an accounting partnership.  Her husband, in
deference to his clients in the confidential nature of his
business and due to the smallness and relative isolation of the
City [as compared to large metropolitan areas], is extremely
unwilling to disclose to his wife the details of his practice.
Due to the nature of his business, this is not so much a problem
with respect to Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) as it is
under Section 87103(c).

     Here her concern is over ascertaining when a conflict exists
and the threshold income requiring (a) disclosure on the reporting form and (b) disqualification from participation in a decision on an item coming before the City Council.

     Her husband owns 100% of a professional corporation.  That
professional corporation engages in business in a professional
membership with another individual.  That partnership is on a
50%-50% ownership basis between her husband's professional corporation and this other individual.

     Given these facts, what is the threshold level of income to
the partnership for (a) disclosure on the reporting form and (b)
for disqualification for participation in a decision coming before the City Council where that decision has a material financial effect on the client, as distinguished from the effect on
the public generally?

     In determining whether June Christensen "knows or has reason
to know" that a conflict exists when a matter comes up for

JAMES R. CHRISTIANSEN
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW


Fair Political Practice Commission
December 31, 1986

decision before the City Council,  would this be satisfied if:

     1.  The City staff would prepare in advance a list of
those applicants who are expected to appear on the next
City Council agenda; and

     2.  She gave that list to her husband prior to  the
meeting and her husband had his client list and the
partnership income reviews in good faith by a member of
the partnership's staff as to the threshold  level of
income who then certified to Councilperson Christiansen
whether any of those on the list had reached the thres-
hold level; and

     3.  She disqualified herself for all that were
reported to her as exceeding the threshold level?

     If you have any questions about the scope of these questions
or need any further clarifications, please do not hesitate to
call me.

                                   Very sincerely yours,

                                   [signature]

                                   James R. Christiansen


JRC/bc

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


January 7, 1987


James R. Christiansen
Attorney and Counselor at Law
1607 Chapala Street
Santa Barbara, CA  93101-2911

Re:  87-019

Dear Mr. Christiansen:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on January 6, 1987 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advices request, you may contact Kathryn E. Donovan, an attorney
in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advices requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days.  You also should be aware that your
letter and our response are public records which may be
disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for
disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

DMG:plh
cc:  June Christiansen


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

sections.analysis:
Disclosure 
Your first question concerns disclosure of income. 
Income 
is broadly defined in the Act. 
with certain exceptions, income 
includes salary, wages and other payments, including any 
community property interest in the income of a spouse. 
Income 
of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income 
of any business entity in which the individual or spouse owns a 
10-percent or greater interest. 
(Section 82030.) 
Section 87207{a) states the general rule that city 
councilmembers must disclose each source of income aggregating 
$250 or more in value. 
However, section 87207(b) provides a 
higher threshold for reporting an official's pro rata share of 
income to a business entity. 
Section 87207(b) provides: 
(b) 
When the filer's pro rata share of income to 
a business entity, including income to a sole 
proprietorship, is required to be reported under this 
article, the statement shall contain: 
(1) 
The name, address, and a general description 
of the business activity of the business entity; 


James R. Christiansen 
February 5, 1987  
(2) 
The name of every person from whom the 
business entity received payments if the filer's pro 
rata share of gross receipts from such person was 
equal to or greater than ten thousand dollars 
($10,000) during a calendar year. 
Thus, Ms. Christensen is required to disclose the name of 
her husband's accounting clients only if her pro rata share of 
the gross receipts from a particular client were $10,000 or 
more during the calendar year. 
Ms. Christensen's pro rata 
share of the gross receipts from the accounting partnership is 
based on her community property interest in her husband's 
income. 
since he is a 50-percent owner of the accounting firm, 
50 percent of its gross receipts are attributed to him. 
Fifty 
percent of those receipts are then attributed to 
Ms. Christensen as her pro rata share. 
Thus, one-fourth of the 
gross receipts of the firm are attributed to her. 
Therefore, 
if a client provides the accounting partnership with $40,000 or 
more in gross receipts in a calendar year, Ms. Christensen's 
share is $10,000 and she must disclose the name of the client 
on her statement of economic interests (Form 721).11 
Ms. Christensen is not required to disclose the name of any 
clients who have provided less than $40,000 to the partnership 
during the calendar year. 
You should also note that Ms. Christensen will be required 
to report her husband's professional corporation on Schedule A 
of Form 721. 
She must report on Schedule C-2 an interest in 
the accounting partnership.Ei 
She must also disclose her 
husband's professional corporation as a source of income on 
Schedule C if her husband's pro rata share of the corporation's 
gross receipts is $500 or more, one-half of which would be 
attributed to her. 
Disqualification 
Your second question concerns Ms. Christensen's duty to 
disqualify herself from participating in governmental decisions 
11 
Because the clients are sources of income to the 
partnership and her husband's corporation, Ms. Christensen may 
list the partnership and the corporation together (i.e. name of 
corporation/name of partnership) on the line for "Name of 
Business Entity" on Schedule H-2 of Form 721. 
Ei 
Investments held directly or indirectly by an 
official's spouse are considered investments of the official. 
(Section 82034.) 
The value to be reported is Ms. Christensen's 
husband's ownership interest in the business entities. 


James R. Christiansen 
February 5, 1987  . 
when she has a conflict of interest. 
Section 87100 prohibits 
any public official from making, participating in, or using her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which 
she knows or has reason to know she has a financial interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally, on, among other things, any source of incom~ 
totaling $250 or more paid to, received by, or promised to the 
official during the 12 months preceding the decision. 
(Section 
87103 (c) .) 
Accordingly, if an official has received at least $250 in 
income from a single source, the income may provide the basis 
for the official's disqualification from decisions affecting 
the source of the income. 
In Ms. Christensen's case, we are 
dealing with income received by or promised to her husband. 
Ms. Christensen's interest in her husband's income is based on 
her community property (i.e., 50-percent) interest. 
Therefore, 
her husband's pro rata share of the gross receipts from a 
client must be over $500 before Ms. Christensen's community 
property share is $250. 
Her husband is a 50-percent owner of 
the accounting partnership; therefore, his pro rata share of 
the gross receipts from any particular client is deemed to be 
50 percent of the total paid. 
Accordingly, a client must pay 
the accounting partnership at least $1,000 before 
Ms. Christensen's share of the income will equal $250. 
If a 
client has paid or promised the accounting partnership $1,000 
or more during the 12 months preceding a city council decision, 
Ms. Christensen must determine whether the decision will have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the client 
that is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
In your letter, you did not discuss any specific decisions 
pending before the city council that might affect one of the 
clients of Ms. Christensen's husband's accounting partnership. 
Therefore, we can provide only the following general guidance 
with regard to Ms. Christensen's duty to disqualify herself 
from participating in city council decisions. 
If, at a future 
date, you have a question about a specific decision, please 
feel free to contact this office. 
When a decision comes before the city council which could 
affect one of the accounting firm's clients who has paid or 
promised the firm $1,000 or more during the preceding 12 
months, it is first necessary to determine whether the client 
will be foreseeably and materially affected by the decision. 
The effect of a decision is considered reasonably foreseeable 
if there is a "substantial likelihood" that it will occur. 


James R. Christiansen 
February 5, 1987  
certainty is not required~ however, if the effect is but a mere 
possibility, is is not reasonably foreseeable. 
(Thorner 
Opinion, 1 FPPC Ops. 198 (No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975), copy 
enclosed.) 
, 
The Commission has adopted guidelines for determining 
whether the effect of a decision will be deemed material. 
Regulation 18702.1(a) (1) (copy enclosed) provides that the 
effect of a decision is material if any person, including a 
business entity, which has been a source of income to the 
official of $250 or more in the preceding 12 months, appears 
before the official in connection with the decision. 
A person 
or business entity "appears before" an official in connection 
with a decision when that person or business entity, either 
personally or through an agent: 
(1) 
Initiates the proceeding in which the 
decision will be made by filing an application, claim, 
appeal, or similar request~ or 
(2) 
Is a named party in the proceeding 
concerning the decision before the official or the 
body on which the official serves. 
Regulation 18702.1(b). 
For example, if Ms. Christensen's husband's client has paid or 
promised to pay the accounting partnership $1,000 or more in 
the past 12 months, and then appears before the city council by 
filing for a conditional use permit, the effect of the decision 
on the client would be considered material. 
Some decisions will affect the clients of the accounting 
partnership even though they are not the applicants for, or 
parties to, the decision. 
For example, if the city council is 
considering raising the fees for building permits within the 
city, that decision may affect one or more of the clients of 
the accounting partnership. 
If the client is a business 
entity, the monetary guidelines contained in Regulation 18702.2 
(copy enclosed) apply for purposes of determining whether the 
effect will be considered material. 
The guidelines in Regulation 18702.2 vary with the size of 
the business entity. 
It appears from your letter that in most 
cases the clients who are business entities would be relatively 
small businesses. 
In that case, if the building permit fee 
decision would increase or decrease the client's expenses by 
$2,500 or more in a fiscal year, the effect is considered 


James R. Christiansen 
February 5, 1987  
material. 
(Regulation 18702.2(g) (2).) 
If the decision were 
one which would foreseeably affect the gross revenues of the 
client, or its assets or liabilities, an increase or decrease 
of $10,000 in a fiscal year is considered material. 
(Regulation 18702.2(g) (1) and (3).) 
There are no similar monetary guidelines for determining 
whether the effect of a decision on a client who is an 
individual will be considered material. 
In that case, if the 
effect on the individual is "significant," it will be 
considered material. 
(Regulation 18702(a), copy enclosed.) 
If Ms. Christensen determines that one of her husband's 
clients has paid $1,000 or more to the partnership during the 
preceding 12 months, and that client would be foreseeably and 
materially affected by a decision pending before the city 
council, she must disqualify herself from participating in the 
decision unless the effect of the decision on the client would 
not be distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally. 
The effect of a governmental decision on an 
official's interests is distinguishable from its effect on the 
public generally unless the decision will affect the official's 
interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all 
members of the public or a significant segment of the public. 
(Regulation 18703, copy enclosed.) 
For Ms. Christensen, the 
"public" is the population of the City of Solvang. 
(See Owen 
Opinion, 2 FPPC Ops. 77, 81 (No. 76-005, June 2, 1976r;-copy 
enclosed. ) 
If a decision would affect Ms. Christensen's husband's 
client in substantially the same manner as it would affect the 
entire City of Solvang, or a significant segment of the city, 
then Ms. Christensen may participate in the decision. 
For 
example, a decision to impose a city sales tax, which will 
affect all residents of the city, does not affect any 
individual client in a different manner than it affects the 
public generally. 
As a result, Ms. Christensen could 
participate in the decision. 
In summary, Ms. Christensen must first determine whether 
the decision would directly or indirectly affect any client of 
her husband's accounting partnership who has paid or promised 
to pay $1,000 or more in the past 12 months. 
If the decision 
could foreseeably and materially affect the client, 
Ms. Christensen must disqualify herself from participating in 
the decision, unless the effect on the client would be 
substantially the same as the effect on the public generally. 


James R. Christiansen 
February 5, 1987  
In your letter, you expressed concern that Ms. Christensen's 
husband is unwilling to disclose to his wife the details of his 
accounting practice. 
His reluctance is reportedly due to the 
confidential nature of his business and the small size and 
relative isolation of Solvang. 
You proposed the following 
method of ensuring that Ms. Christensen will know when to 
disqualify herself from a decision affecting one of her 
husband's clients: 
1. 
The city staff would prepare in advance a list of 
those applicants who are expected to appear on the next 
city council agenda. 
2. 
She would give that list to her husband prior to 
the meeting. 
Her husband would have his client list and 
the partnership income reviewed with due diligence by a 
member of the partnership's staff as to the threshold level 
of income (i.e., $1,000). 
Ms. Christensen would then be 
advised whether any of those on the list had reached the 
threshold level. 
3. 
She would disqualify herself for all clients that 
were reported to her as exceeding the threshold of $1,000. 
Your proposed method of determining which clients could 
create potential conflicts of interest is basically a good 
approach. 
We have a few comments, however. 
First, as we 
discussed above, city council decisions may directly or 
indirectly affect more than the applicants. 
For example, if a 
decision were to affect real property adjacent to property 
owned by one of Ms. Christensen's husband's clients, and the 
effect on the client's real property would be foreseeable, 
material, and distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally, then Ms. Christensen would be required to disqualify 
herself from participating in the decision. 
Therefore, we 
suggest that the city staff provide Ms. Christensen with a 
description of the subject matter of the decisions pending 
before the city council, in addition to providing a list of the 
applicants. 
You should note, however, that when a client is 
affected by a decision, but is not the applicant, 
Ms. Christensen's disqualification would not be automatic. 
Her 
disqualification is required only if there would be a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the client, 
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
As you discussed in your letter, Ms. Christensen is 
required to disqualify herself from participating in a decision 
if she knows or has reason to know of her financial interest in 


James R. Christiansen 
February 5, 1987  
the decision. 
(Section 87100.) 
The approach you have 
suggested would be evidence of Ms. Christensen's good faith 
effort to comply with the disqualification requirements of the 
Act.§! 
Nevertheless, Ms. Christensen should be informed that 
her responsibility for disclosure and disqualification cannot 
be transferred to a staff person in her husband's firm. 
If she 
becomes aware of her husband's clients through other means, 
such as during a social function or during conversations with 
her husband, she may also know or have reason to know of her 
financial interest in a particular decision.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
When must Ms. Christensen disclose the names of her 
husband's clients on her statement of economic interests? 
2. 
When must Ms. Christensen disqualify herself from 
participating in a decision before the city council that could 
affect her husband's client?

CONCLUSION: 1. 
Ms. Christensen must disclose on her statement of 
economic interests the name of any client of her husband's 
accounting partnership who has provided $40,000 or more in 
gross receipts to the partnership during the calendar year. 
2. 
Ms. Christensen must disqualify herself from 
participating in any decision which would foreseeably and 
materially affect a client of her husband's accounting 
partnership who has provided or promised $1,000 or more in 
gross receipts to the partnership during the 12 months 
preceding the decision, if the effect of the decision on the 
client would be distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission SUPERSEDED IN PART February 5, 1987 by: 95-213 James R. Christiansen 1607 Chapala Street Santa Barbara, CA 93101-2911 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-87-019 Dear Mr. Christiansen: You have requested advice on behalf of June Christensen, a member of the City Council of Solvang, regarding her duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Your letter states only a general question; it does not seek advice concerning a specific decision pending before the city council. Therefore, we consider it to be a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2/ FACTS Ms. Christensen's husband is a 50-percent partner in an accounting partnership. He owns his 50-percent share in the partnership through a professional corporation, of which he owns 100 percent.3/ 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Administrative Code Section 18000, et seq. All references to Regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Administrative Code. 2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 3/ We have assumed that there is no separate property agreement between Ms. Christensen and her husband. Your letter does not mention such an agreement; therefore, this letter is based on the assumption that any investments and income in question are community property. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 James R. Christiansen February 5, 1987 Page 2 QUESTIONS 1. When must Ms. Christensen disclose the names of her husband's clients on her statement of economic interests? 2. When must Ms. Christensen disqualify herself from participating in a decision before the city council that could affect her husband's client? CONCLUSIONS 1. Ms. Christensen must disclose on her statement of economic interests the name of any client of her husband's accounting partnership who has provided $40,000 or more in gross receipts to the partnership during the calendar year. 2. Ms. Christensen must disqualify herself from participating in any decision which would foreseeably and materially affect a client of her husband's accounting partnership who has provided or promised $1,000 or more in gross receipts to the partnership during the 12 months preceding the decision, if the effect of the decision on the client would be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. ANALYSIS Disclosure Your first question concerns disclosure of income. Income is broadly defined in the Act. With certain exceptions, income includes salary, wages and other payments, including any community property interest in the income of a spouse. Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity in which the individual or spouse owns a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030.) Section 87207(a) states the general rule that city councilmembers must disclose each source of income aggregating $250 or more in
